source_path: /Users/hal.hildebrand/Downloads/delos-papers/Aging Bloom Filter with Two Active Buffers for Dynamic Sets-1.pdf

fields.problem_formulation: Standard Bloom filters support only static sets, but networking applications increasingly need membership queries over dynamic sets in small SRAM, requiring an aging scheme that selectively deletes stale data while keeping the false positive rate within a fixed bound.
fields.proposed_method: The paper proposes A2 (active-active) buffering, which splits the Bloom filter memory into two simultaneously active buffers (active1 and active2). New data are inserted into active1; on a hit in active2, the element is copied into active1; when active1 fills up, active2 is flushed and the two buffers swap roles. Per-buffer false positive rate is set to fa = 1 - sqrt(1-f) so the combined rate stays at the target f.
experimental_datasets: NLANR PMA packet header traces from the OC192c Packet-over-SONET link between Indianapolis (IPLS) and Kansas City (KSCY), collected June 1, 2004
experimental_baselines: Stable Bloom Filter (SBF, Deng and Rafiei)
fields.experimental_results: On real Internet traces, A2 buffering achieved a higher average cache hit ratio than double buffering, cold cache, and SBF across varying false positive ratios (10^-9 to 10^-3) and memory sizes (1-16 KB), while incurring fewer resets than double buffering; theoretical analysis shows A2 can store up to ~2x as many elements as double buffering in best cases and at least ~nd+1 in worst cases.

extras.venue: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering, Vol. 22, No. 1, January 2010
extras.author: MyungKeun Yoon
extras.affiliation: Korea Financial Telecommunications and Clearings Institute (KFTC)
key_theorems: na approx nd for small f

fields.problem_formulation: Bloom filters traditionally support only static sets, but networking applications increasingly require membership queries over dynamic sets where stale data must be evicted (aged) within a fixed, small SRAM memory budget while maintaining a bounded false positive ratio.
fields.proposed_method: The paper proposes Active-Active (A2) buffering, an aging scheme that splits the Bloom filter memory into two simultaneously active buffers (active1 and active2). New items are inserted into active1; on a hit in active2 the item is copied forward to active1, and when active1 fills up, active2 is flushed and the buffers swap roles, so both buffers always hold distinct useful data.
experimental_datasets: NLANR PMA packet header traces from the OC192c Packet-over-SONET link between Indianapolis (IPLS) and Kansas City (KSCY), collected June 1, 2004
fields.experimental_results: On real Internet traces, A2 buffering achieves a higher average cache hit ratio than double buffering, cold cache, and SBF across allowed false positive ratios from 10^-9 to 10^-3 and memory sizes from 1 to 16 KB, while incurring fewer resets than double buffering and avoiding the full-memory load spikes of cold cache.

extras.venue: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering, Vol. 22, No. 1, January 2010 (Concise Papers)
extras.author: MyungKeun Yoon
extras.affiliation: Korea Financial Telecommunications and Clearings Institute (KFTC)

source_path: /Users/hal.hildebrand/Downloads/delos-papers/Aging Bloom Filter with Two Active Buffers for Dynamic Sets-1.pdf

fields.problem_formulation: Bloom filters are designed for static sets, but many networking applications require membership queries over dynamic sets where stale data must be evicted (aged) under tight SRAM memory constraints. The paper addresses how to age Bloom filters efficiently so that more recent data can be retained without exceeding a fixed memory size and false-positive ratio.
fields.proposed_method: The authors propose Active-Active (A2) buffering, an aging scheme that maintains two simultaneously active Bloom filter buffers (active1 and active2) each of size m/2. New items are inserted into active1; on a hit in active2 the item is copied into active1, and when active1 fills up, active2 is flushed and the two buffers swap roles. Per-buffer false positive rate is set to fa = 1 - sqrt(1 - f) so the combined rate matches the target f.
experimental_datasets: NLANR PMA OC192c Packet-over-SONET trace, Indianapolis (IPLS) to Kansas City (KSCY) link, collected June 1, 2004
experimental_baselines: Stable Bloom Filter (SBF, Deng and Rafiei)
fields.experimental_results: On real Internet traces, A2 buffering achieves a higher average cache hit ratio than double buffering, cold cache, and SBF across allowed false-positive ratios (10^-9 to 10^-3) and memory sizes (1-16 KB), while incurring fewer resets than double buffering. Theoretically, A2 buffering can store up to 2*nd data versus nd for double buffering in best-case input patterns, and at least nd+1 in worst-case patterns.

extras.venue: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering, Vol. 22, No. 1, January 2010
extras.author: MyungKeun Yoon (KFTC)
theoretical_results: Theorem 2: na ~ nd for small f

fields.problem_formulation: Byzantine Fault-Tolerant state machine replication (BFT SMR) protocols, while a natural fit for permissioned blockchains, exhibit subtle but fundamental limitations beyond consensus scalability: their internal logs do not provide a durable, self-verifiable blockchain, and their replica reconfiguration mechanisms typically depend on a centralized trusted party and can permit forks from compromised ex-members.
fields.proposed_method: The authors propose SMARTCHAIN, a permissioned blockchain platform built on BFT-SMaRt that introduces a consensus-agnostic blockchain storage layer transforming the SMR log into a self-verifiable chain of immutable blocks (with headers, transaction results, and certificates) and adds an optional PERSIST phase to achieve 0-Persistence durability. It also introduces a decentralized reconfiguration protocol that decouples permanent keys from per-view consensus keys (forgetting protocol) so that ex-members cannot forge forks, allowing autonomous join/leave under application-specific policies.
experimental_datasets: SMaRtCoin digital coin workload (UTXO-model MINT and SPEND transactions, single-input/single-output)
Synthetic state of 8 million UTXOs (~1GB), representing ~10% of Bitcoin's UTXO set at the time
experimental_baselines: BFT-SMaRt (with durability layer) running SMaRtCoin natively
fields.experimental_results: SMARTCHAIN reaches ~12.5k tx/s (strong, 0-Persistence) and ~14.5k tx/s (weak) with signatures and synchronous writes on 4 replicas, improving the SMaRtCoin-on-BFT-SMaRt baseline by ~8x and outperforming Tendermint by ~8x and Hyperledger Fabric by ~33x in throughput, while also showing low-impact reconfigurations and fast (~60s) state transfer for a 1GB state.

extras.venue: 50th IEEE/IFIP International Conference on Dependable Systems and Networks (DSN 2020), preprint on arXiv (2004.14527)
extras.system_model: Eventually synchronous, Byzantine faults, dynamic replica groups with infinite arrival and bounded concurrency
key_mechanisms: Self-verifiable block structure (header + body + certificate of >=2f+1 signatures)
Decoupled checkpoints stored outside the chain, referenced by block number
Per-view consensus keypairs with forgetting protocol to prevent reconfiguration forks
Application-specific join/leave policies via signed quorum acceptance
extras.hardware: 14 Dell PowerEdge R410 servers, 2x quad-core Xeon E5520 with HT, 32GB RAM, 1Gbps switched network, Ubuntu 16.04, JRE 1.8

fields.problem_formulation: Classical Byzantine Fault-Tolerant state machine replication (BFT SMR) protocols, when used as the basis of permissioned blockchains, exhibit subtle limitations beyond consensus scalability: their write-ahead logs are not self-verifiable durable blockchains, they provide only weak ('external') durability that can lose committed transactions on full crashes, and they rely on a centralized trusted party for replica-set reconfiguration — leaving the door open to forks from compromised ex-members.
fields.proposed_method: The authors propose SMARTCHAIN, a blockchain layer built on top of BFT-SMaRt that (1) turns the SMR operation log into a self-verifiable chain of blocks containing transactions, results, headers and a quorum certificate, with decoupled asynchronous block writing and an extra PERSIST signature round to deliver 0-Persistence ('strong') durability; (2) stores state snapshots outside the chain and links them via block headers; and (3) introduces a decentralized reconfiguration protocol where replicas use ephemeral per-view consensus key pairs (a forgetting protocol) and application-policy-driven join/leave/remove transactions, preventing forks by removed or later-compromised members.
experimental_datasets: SMaRtCoin (UTXO-style digital coin workload, Bitcoin/FabCoin-inspired, MINT and single-input single-output SPEND transactions)
Synthetic state of 8 million UTXOs (~1 GB, ~10% of Bitcoin's UTXO set) used in the reconfiguration/crash/recovery experiment
experimental_baselines: BFT-SMaRt (plain, with the Dura-SMaRt durability layer, running SMaRtCoin natively)
Hyperledger Fabric (with a BFT ordering service)
fields.experimental_results: With strong (0-Persistence) durability and signatures+synchronous writes on 4 replicas, SMARTCHAIN sustains ~12.5k tx/s at ~210 ms latency (weak variant ~14.5k tx/s) — about 8× the throughput of running SMaRtCoin directly on BFT-SMaRt, and roughly 8× Tendermint (~1.6k tx/s) and 33× Hyperledger Fabric (~381 tx/s); reconfigurations, crashes and recoveries are handled with only modest, transient throughput dips, and checkpointing greatly accelerates state catch-up for joining/recovering replicas.

extras.venue: 50th IEEE/IFIP International Conference on Dependable Systems and Networks (DSN 2020)
reconfiguration_features: per-view consensus key pairs with forgetting protocol to prevent forks
ablation_dimensions: signature verification (sequential vs parallel vs none)
storage strategy (sync vs async vs memory-only)

experimental_setup.machines: 14 Dell PowerEdge R410, 2x quad-core Intel Xeon E5520 @2.27GHz w/ HT (16 threads), 32 GB RAM, 146 GB SCSI HDD

extras.code_release: Not explicitly stated in the paper text

fields.problem_formulation: Byzantine Fault-Tolerant state machine replication (BFT SMR) protocols are a natural fit for permissioned blockchains, but beyond the well-studied scalability of consensus, there are subtle gaps between SMR and blockchain requirements—specifically around maintaining a durable, self-verifiable ledger and supporting decentralized reconfiguration of the replica set—that lead to performance and correctness limitations when blockchain applications are built naively on top of SMR libraries.
fields.proposed_method: The authors identify three SMR-vs-blockchain gaps (performance of naive blockchain-on-SMR, lack of self-verifiable durable ledger, and centralized reconfiguration) and propose protocol-agnostic mechanisms to address them: an efficient blockchain storage layer that turns the SMR operation log into a chain of self-verifiable blocks (with headers, transaction results, certificates, and decoupled checkpoints), an extra PERSIST round to upgrade durability from external/1-Persistence to 0-Persistence (strong variant), and a decentralized reconfiguration protocol that uses per-view consensus key pairs with key forgetting to prevent forks by removed members. They implement these techniques in SMARTCHAIN, a permissioned blockchain platform built on BFT-SMART.
experimental_datasets: SMaRtCoin (UTXO-based digital coin workload with MINT and SPEND transactions, inspired by Bitcoin/FabCoin; up to 8 million UTXOs, ~1GB state, ~10% of Bitcoin's UTXO count)
experimental_baselines: BFT-SMART (with Dura-SMaRt durability layer, no blockchain)
SMaRtCoin naively on top of BFT-SMART
fields.experimental_results: SMARTCHAIN improves SMaRtCoin throughput by ~8x over a naive BFT-SMART implementation (reaching ~12k tx/s strong and ~14.5k tx/s weak persistence with signatures and synchronous writes at n=4), and outperforms Tendermint by ~8x (1.6k tx/s) and Hyperledger Fabric by ~33x (381 tx/s) under strong durability, while sustaining throughput across reconfigurations, crashes, and recoveries with checkpoint-accelerated state transfer.

extras.venue: 50th IEEE/IFIP International Conference on Dependable Systems and Networks (DSN 2020)
ablation_dimensions: synchronous vs asynchronous vs in-memory writes
reconfiguration_features: per-view consensus key forgetting to prevent forks
extras.hardware: 14 Dell PowerEdge R410 servers, 32GB RAM, 2x quad-core Xeon E5520 (16 HW threads), 146GB SCSI HDD, 1Gbps switched network, Ubuntu 16.04, JRE 1.8
extras.fault_model: f Byzantine failures with n>=3f+1; eventually synchronous; recoverable crashes with stable storage
extras.code_release: not explicitly stated in the paper

fields.problem_formulation: Existing modular transformations from Byzantine consensus to BFT state machine replication require more communication steps than monolithic BFT SMR protocols, creating a gap between modularity and latency optimality. The paper asks whether a modular BFT SMR protocol can achieve the optimal number of communication steps (matching PBFT) while explicitly using a consensus primitive at its core.
fields.proposed_method: The paper introduces MOD-SMART, a modular BFT state machine replication algorithm built on top of a new 'grey-box' consensus abstraction called Validated and Provable Consensus (VP-Consensus), which augments standard Byzantine consensus with an external validity predicate, a cryptographic decision proof, and explicit leader/timeout interfaces. MOD-SMART uses VP-Consensus to avoid an extra reliable broadcast step, handling request timeouts and leader changes at the SMR layer while delegating intra-consensus inconsistencies to the consensus module. Existing leader-driven partially-synchronous Byzantine consensus algorithms (e.g., Paxos-style, PBFT-like) can be adapted to VP-Consensus with simple modifications.
fields.experimental_results: The paper is theoretical and presents no empirical experiments; the headline analytical result is that MOD-SMART adds at most one communication step to the underlying consensus to implement total order broadcast, yielding a latency- and resiliency-optimal modular BFT SMR with the same number of communication steps and cryptographic operations as PBFT in the synchronous, correct-leader case.

extras.venue: EDCC 2012 (referenced via acknowledgments to EDCC'12 reviewers)
optimizations_discussed: checkpoints and state transfer to bound log
MAC vectors instead of digital signatures

fields.problem_formulation: Existing modular Byzantine fault-tolerant (BFT) state machine replication (SMR) protocols built atop black-box Byzantine consensus primitives require more communication steps than monolithic BFT SMR protocols, while monolithic protocols sacrifice modularity. The paper asks whether a modular BFT SMR transformation can achieve the optimal number of communication steps (matching PBFT) while still using a well-defined consensus primitive at its core.
fields.proposed_method: The authors introduce MOD-SMART, a modular BFT SMR algorithm built atop a new 'grey-box' consensus abstraction called Validated and Provable Consensus (VP-Consensus), which augments classical Byzantine consensus with an external validity predicate, a cryptographic decision proof, and an externally-driven leader/timeout interface. By exposing this richer interface, MOD-SMART avoids the reliable broadcast layer used by prior modular reductions and adds only two communication steps (client request + reply) on top of the underlying leader-driven Byzantine consensus. The paper also describes the simple modifications needed to turn existing leader-driven Paxos-style Byzantine consensus protocols into VP-Consensus implementations.
experimental_baselines: Cachin et al. reduction (echo broadcast + signatures)
Guerraoui et al. (Next 700 BFT Protocols / Aliph)
fields.experimental_results: This is a theoretical paper with no empirical evaluation; the headline result is that MOD-SMART is the first modular BFT SMR transformation that is latency-optimal, requiring only the number of communication steps of the underlying Byzantine consensus plus two (matching PBFT's message pattern in synchronous, correct-leader executions), while remaining resiliency-optimal at n >= 3f+1.

extras.affiliation: University of Lisbon, Faculty of Sciences, LaSIGE
extras.system_model: eventually synchronous, n >= 3f+1 Byzantine replicas, authenticated point-to-point channels, digital signatures / MACs
key_contributions: Definition of Validated and Provable Consensus (VP-Consensus) primitive with External Validity and External Provability properties
MOD-SMART transformation from VP-Consensus to BFT SMR with latency-optimal step count
Sketch of modifications to existing leader-driven Paxos-style Byzantine consensus protocols to satisfy the VP-Consensus interface
Correctness proofs (safety Theorem 1, liveness Theorem 2 via Lemmata 1-3) in the Appendix
optimizations_discussed: Periodic local checkpoints + state transfer to bound decision log
MAC vectors instead of digital signatures on critical path (à la PBFT)

fields.problem_formulation: Existing modular transformations from Byzantine consensus to BFT state machine replication require more communication steps than monolithic BFT SMR protocols (e.g., PBFT), creating a gap between modularity and latency optimality. The paper asks whether a BFT SMR protocol can achieve PBFT-like optimal communication-step latency while explicitly using a consensus primitive at its core.
fields.proposed_method: The authors introduce MOD-SMART, a modular BFT state machine replication algorithm built atop a new 'grey-box' consensus primitive called Validated and Provable Consensus (VP-Consensus), which augments classical Byzantine consensus with External Validity (a predicate γ on decided values) and External Provability (a cryptographic proof Γ of decisions), plus an interface for externally driven leader changes and timeouts. MOD-SMART avoids the reliable broadcast layer used by prior modular reductions by leveraging VP-Consensus's richer interface for synchronization and leader change, requiring only two extra communication steps beyond consensus (client request + reply). Existing leader-driven partially-synchronous Byzantine consensus protocols (e.g., PBFT-style, Paxos-based) can be adapted into VP-Consensus with simple modifications.
fields.experimental_results: This is a theoretical paper with no empirical evaluation; the headline result is that MOD-SMART is the first modular BFT SMR transformation that is latency-optimal, requiring only the number of consensus communication steps plus two (matching PBFT) while preserving a clean separation between the SMR layer and the consensus primitive, with correctness proven in the appendix.

extras.resilience: optimal: 3f+1 replicas tolerate f Byzantine faults
extras.key_abstraction: Validated and Provable Consensus (VP-Consensus) with External Validity and External Provability properties
optimizations_discussed: checkpoints and state transfer to bound log size
MAC vectors instead of digital signatures for client requests and consensus proofs

fields.problem_formulation: Membership management in overlay networks is vulnerable to Byzantine attackers who can falsely report correct members as crashed, keep crashed members listed as live, or bias topology to eclipse correct nodes — undermining any higher-level fault tolerance built on the overlay.
fields.proposed_method: Fireflies organizes members into a pseudorandom mesh of k=2t+1 verifiable membership rings (via SHA-based hashing of identities and ring IDs) so that each member is monitored by multiple pseudorandom predecessors. It combines an accusation/rebuttal scheme with signed notes (using a CA-issued certificate per member), an adaptive ping-based crash detector, and a secure gossip dissemination layer to maintain a full membership view that is eventually consistent among correct members.
experimental_datasets: Simulated Fireflies networks (N from 20 to 160, 6h runs with churn MTTF=MTTR=6h)
fields.experimental_results: Simulations show membership/network overhead scales linearly with N under both aggressive and passive attacks at up to 10% corrupt members, with no view divergence at pcorrupt≤0.10; on PlanetLab with 10% aggressive + 10% passive attackers, mean outbound gossip bandwidth stayed below ~250 Bps per correct member (peaks ~500 Bps during mass kill/restart), demonstrating Fireflies effectively thwarts attacks at modest overhead.

extras.venue: ACM Transactions on Computer Systems, Vol. 33, No. 2, Article 5 (May 2015)
extras.prior_version: EuroSys 2006 paper by Johansen et al.; extended journal version
extras.implementation: Python + C++ extensions on Twisted; OpenSSL with NIST P-224 ECC; SSL/TCP for gossip, UDP for pings; partitioned set reconciliation (Minsky & Trachtenberg)
theoretical_results: Chernoff bound: P[majority corrupt monitors] = exp(-O(k))
k = Θ(log N) suffices for O(1) expected unfortunate nodes
Diameter of correct-member graph is O(log n / log(npn))
limitations_noted: Views are eventually/probabilistically consistent, never strongly consistent
Cannot prevent attackers from joining (Sybil mitigation assumed via CA)

fields.problem_formulation: Overlay networks are vulnerable to Byzantine attacks on their membership management mechanisms, where corrupt members can falsely report correct members as crashed, keep crashed members listed as live, or bias topology to eclipse correct nodes. The paper addresses how to maintain secure, scalable, and consistent full membership views in overlay networks despite such intrusions.
fields.proposed_method: Fireflies organizes members into a pseudorandom mesh of k=2t+1 membership rings (derived from secure hashes of member and ring identifiers) so that each member is monitored by multiple verifiable predecessors. Members exchange signed notes, accusations, and rebuttals over a gossip-based broadcast channel, with bitmap masks to disable misbehaving monitors and an adaptive ping-based crash detector that tunes its timeout threshold to measured packet-loss rate. The protocol gives every correct member a full view of all live members and a low-diameter neighbor graph suited for gossip.
experimental_datasets: Custom discrete-event simulator (20–160 simulated members, 6h runs, MTTF=MTTR=6h exponential churn)
PlanetLab deployment (376 nodes, March 12–14, 2012, with induced 25% kill/restart event)
fields.experimental_results: In simulation, network load (rate of notes and accusations) scales linearly with membership size and remains low even with 10% aggressive or passive attackers, with no view inconsistencies observed at pcorrupt=0.10. On PlanetLab with 376 nodes, mean outbound TCP gossip bandwidth stayed below ~250 Bps per correct member, peaking near 500 Bps during mass kill/restart events, demonstrating practical overhead under real wide-area churn and attack.

extras.venue: ACM Transactions on Computer Systems (TOCS), Vol. 33, No. 2, Article 5, May 2015
extras.extends_prior_work: EuroSys 2006 paper by Johansen et al. and Johansen's 2007 PhD dissertation
attack_models_evaluated: aggressive (false accusations, refuse to forward notes)
passive (never accuse, suppress accusations of crashed members)
extras.implementation: Python + C++ on Twisted, OpenSSL with NIST P-224 ECC, X.509 certificates, partitioned set reconciliation (Minsky-Trachtenberg)
limitations: cannot bound fraction of crashed members
theoretical_results: Chernoff bound: k=Θ(log N) rings keep expected unfortunate nodes O(1)
Erdős–Rényi connectivity bound for gossip mesh

fields.problem_formulation: Overlay networks are vulnerable to Byzantine attackers who can target the membership maintenance mechanism to falsely report correct members as crashed, keep crashed members as live, or bias topology (eclipse attacks), thereby compromising the entire overlay.
fields.proposed_method: Fireflies organizes members into a verifiable pseudorandom mesh of k membership rings using a secure hash on member and ring identifiers, assigns monitors deterministically through these rings, uses a signed note/accusation/rebuttal scheme with adaptive pinging for crash detection, and disseminates membership updates via a gossip mesh built over the same ring structure, providing each member with a full membership view and a low-diameter connected subgraph of correct members.
fields.experimental_results: Simulations with up to 160 members and 10% aggressive/passive attackers showed network overhead scaling linearly with membership size and no view divergence among correct members; PlanetLab deployment on 376 nodes with induced 25% churn and 20% corrupt members sustained mean outbound bandwidth below ~250 Bps per correct member (peaking near 500 Bps during mass churn events).

extras.venue: ACM Transactions on Computer Systems (TOCS), Vol. 33, No. 2, Article 5, May 2015
extras.extends_prior_work: EuroSys 2006 paper by Johansen et al. and Johansen's 2007 PhD dissertation
extras.implementation: Python + C++ extensions on Twisted, OpenSSL with NIST P-224 ECC, X.509 certificates, set reconciliation via Minsky-Trachtenberg
extras.theoretical_contribution: Chernoff-bound analysis showing k = Θ(log N) rings suffice to keep expected number of unfortunate (majority-corrupt-monitored) nodes constant

fields.problem_formulation: The Merkle tree, widely used for verifying authenticity and integrity of data blocks in applications such as Blockchain, IoT, and privacy-preserving computing, is computationally costly, consumes significant memory, and generates excessive network traffic because partial block verification requires fetching multiple hash values over the network.
fields.proposed_method: The authors propose HEX-BLOOM, a replacement for the Merkle tree composed of two parts: LinkedHashX, a deterministic structure that hashes all data blocks and XORs them together to form a compact root for final correctness verification, and a Bloom Filter that stores all block hashes to support approximate per-block authenticity/integrity checks. A user downloads only the LinkedHashX root and the Bloom Filter once, then verifies each downloaded block locally against the Bloom Filter and confirms overall correctness by reconstructing the LinkedHashX root.
fields.experimental_results: The paper provides only analytical (asymptotic) comparisons rather than empirical experiments, showing HEX-BLOOM achieves O(kL) construction, O(k) per-block verification, O(1) communication cost, and μ-bit extra space versus the Merkle tree's O(n), O(log n), O(L), and O(n), respectively, claiming HEX-BLOOM outperforms the Merkle tree in every aspect.

fields.problem_formulation: The Merkle tree, widely used for verifying authenticity and integrity of data blocks in blockchain, IoT, smart grid, and privacy-preserving computing, is computationally costly, consumes large memory, and creates excessive network traffic because per-block verification requires fetching partial hash values over the network.
fields.proposed_method: The authors propose HEX-BLOOM, a two-part replacement for the Merkle tree combining (1) LinkedHashX, a deterministic structure that hashes each block with a cryptographic hash and XORs all block hashes into a single root (with a second parallel hash chain for collision resistance), and (2) a Bloom Filter into which all block hashes are inserted to enable approximate per-block authenticity/integrity verification without network access. A user downloads the LinkedHashX root and Bloom Filter once, verifies each block against the Bloom Filter locally, and finally reconstructs the LinkedHashX root for deterministic confirmation.
fields.experimental_results: The paper presents an analytical (complexity-theoretic) comparison rather than empirical experiments, showing HEX-BLOOM achieves O(kL) construction, O(k) per-block verification, and O(1) network access versus the Merkle tree's O(n) construction, O(log_m n) per-block verification, and O(L) network access, with substantially lower extra space (μ bits vs O(n)).

extras.venue: Cryptology ePrint Archive (also submitted to IEEE)
extras.affiliation: National Institute of Technology Silchar, India
extras.collision_analysis: Birthday paradox bound; log L parallel LinkedHashX roots discussed for higher security
extras.known_drawback: False positive in Bloom Filter can cause undetected per-block error until full LinkedHashX reconstruction reveals mismatch, requiring full re-download

fields.problem_formulation: The Merkle tree, widely used for verifying authenticity and integrity of data blocks in applications such as blockchain and privacy-preserving computing, is computationally costly, consumes large memory, and creates excessive network traffic because partial verification of each block requires fetching multiple hash values over the network.
fields.proposed_method: The authors propose HEX-BLOOM, a replacement for the Merkle tree that combines (1) LinkedHashX, a deterministic structure that hashes each data block and XORs all hashes together to produce one (or more) compact roots, and (2) a Bloom Filter that stores all block hashes for approximate per-block authenticity/integrity checks. A verifier downloads only the LinkedHashX root(s) and the Bloom Filter once, checks each downloaded block against the Bloom Filter, and finally reconstructs and compares the LinkedHashX root for deterministic verification.
fields.experimental_results: The paper presents an analytical (complexity-theoretic) comparison rather than empirical experiments, arguing HEX-BLOOM beats the Merkle tree on every axis: O(kL) vs O(n) construction, O(k) vs O(log_m n) per-block verification, O(1) vs O(L) communication cost, and lower extra space (μ Bloom-filter bits vs O(n)).

extras.venue: Cryptology ePrint Archive (preprint, submitted to IEEE)
extras.affiliation: National Institute of Technology Silchar, India
extras.evaluation_type: theoretical / complexity analysis (no empirical experiments reported)
extras.security_analysis: birthday-paradox collision probability discussed; multiple LinkedHashX roots (up to log L) recommended for higher security

fields.problem_formulation: Unauthorized access to private resources remains a critical cybersecurity challenge, and traditional static access control mechanisms and MFA schemes are insufficient against advanced exploitation techniques such as session hijacking, which takes over already-established sessions to bypass authentication and conventional access control.
fields.proposed_method: The paper proposes MFAz, a multi-factor authorization scheme that uses fine-grained attribute-based access control rules (ARs) as the first factor and verification points (VPs) systematically derived from historically granted accesses and nonces (e.g., timestamps) as the second factor. Access is granted only when both ARs and VPs verify successfully. The scheme is implemented in C/C++ using the MIRACL library with SHA-256, employs a Bloom filter for storage/runtime efficiency, and uses blockchain (Ganache testnet) to immutably store ARs and VPs in a decentralized, tamper-proof manner.
fields.experimental_results: On a smart-city IIoT testbed with a Gateway, PC, and Raspberry Pi, MFAz correctly granted/denied access in all conventional and session-hijacking test cases with no false positives or negatives, while VP generation averaged 0.53 ms (Gateway), 1.08 ms (PC), and 4.54 ms (RPi) and VP verification averaged 0.92 / 1.27 / 4.10 ms respectively; the Bloom filter compressed 1000 VP entries (32,000 bytes) into ~1255 bytes, demonstrating practicality even on resource-constrained devices.

extras.affiliation: Singapore University of Technology and Design
extras.testbed: IIoT-based smart-city testbed with edge gateways, PCs, RPis, IoT cloud platform; protocols HTTP/HTTPS, TCP, UDP, MQTT
extras.novelty_claim: First formal multi-factor authorization (MFAz) scheme, orthogonal to MFA
threat_model_coverage: session sidejacking (out of scope due to TLS assumption)
extras.funding: National Research Foundation Singapore, Cities of Tomorrow R&D Programme (CoT-V2-2021-1)

fields.problem_formulation: Unauthorized access to private resources remains a critical cybersecurity challenge, and traditional static access control mechanisms are insufficient against advanced exploitation strategies such as session hijacking, where attackers take over established sessions to gain illicit access.
fields.proposed_method: The authors propose MFAz, a multi-factor authorization scheme that uses fine-grained attribute-based access control rules (ARs) as the first factor and verification points (VPs) systematically generated from historically granted accesses and nonces as the second factor. Access is granted only when both factors are verified. The scheme is implemented using bloom filters for runtime/storage efficiency and blockchain (via smart contracts) for tamper-proof, decentralized authorization decisions.
fields.experimental_results: On a smart-city testbed with Gateway, PC, and Raspberry Pi devices, MFAz correctly granted/denied all legitimate and malicious attempts with no false positives or negatives; average VP generation took 0.53/1.08/4.54 ms and VP verification took 0.92/1.27/4.10 ms on Gateway/PC/RPi respectively, while the bloom filter compressed 32,000 bytes of VPs into just 1255 bytes.

extras.testbed: IIoT-based smart city testbed with edge gateways, PCs, Raspberry Pi 4 Model B
extras.implementation: C/C++ with MIRACL cryptography library, SHA-256 hash, Ganache local Ethereum blockchain
extras.bloom_filter_config: ~1000 entries, 1% false positive rate, 9585 bits, 7 hash functions
extras.threat_model: Conventional access control attacks and session hijacking (session fixation, XSS, brute forcing, but not sidejacking due to assumed secure channel)
extras.affiliation: Singapore University of Technology and Design
extras.funding: National Research Foundation Singapore, Cities of Tomorrow R&D Programme (CoT Award COT-V2-2021-1)

fields.problem_formulation: Unauthorized access to private resources remains a critical cybersecurity challenge, and traditional static access control policies and multi-factor authentication schemes are insufficient to defend against advanced exploitation strategies such as session hijacking, where attackers take over an already established session to gain unauthorized access.
fields.proposed_method: The paper proposes MFAz, a multi-factor authorization scheme that uses fine-grained attribute-based access control rules (ARs) as the first authorization factor and verification points (VPs) systematically generated from historically granted accesses and nonces (e.g., timestamps) as the second factor. Access is granted only when both factors verify successfully. The scheme leverages Bloom filters for runtime and storage efficiency and blockchain (with smart contracts) to immutably store ARs and VPs in a decentralized, tamper-proof manner.
fields.experimental_results: On a smart-city IIoT testbed across an edge gateway, PC, and Raspberry Pi, the scheme correctly granted all legitimate accesses and denied all malicious attempts (no false positives or negatives) against conventional and session hijacking attacks, with average VP generation times of 0.53 ms (Gateway), 1.08 ms (PC), and 4.54 ms (RPi), VP verification times of 0.92/1.27/4.10 ms respectively, and the Bloom filter compressing 32,000 bytes of VPs into ~1255 bytes.

extras.implementation: C/C++ with MIRACL cryptography library, SHA-256 hash, Ganache local Ethereum testnet
extras.testbed: IIoT smart-city testbed with ADLINK MVP-510A edge gateway, Dell XPS 13 9315 PC, Raspberry Pi 4 Model B
extras.bloom_filter_config: ~1000 entries, 1% false positive rate, ~9585 bits, 7 hash functions
extras.funding: Singapore National Research Foundation, Cities of Tomorrow R&D Programme (CoT-V2-2021-1)
extras.novelty_claim: First formal multi-factor authorization scheme (orthogonal to MFA)
